h1Title: PTSD: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) develops in approximately 20% of people exposed to a traumatic event, and affects an estimated 3.9-5.6% of the global adult population at any point in time. The disorder follows exposure to actual or threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence, producing four clusters of symptoms: re-experiencing (flashbacks, nightmares), hyperarousal, avoidance, and negative alterations in cognition and mood. Women develop PTSD at approximately twice the rate of men following equivalent exposures. Without treatment, PTSD runs a chronic course in more than 40% of those affected. Two trauma-focused psychological therapies — prolonged exposure (PE) and trauma-focused cognitive-behavioural therapy (TF-CBT) — along with EMDR, have Level A evidence for PTSD and produce remission in 50-70% of treated patients.

keyStats.prevalence: Lifetime prevalence 6.1-8% in the United States (National Comorbidity Survey); 3.9-5.6% point prevalence globally (Kessler et al. 2017)
keyStats.demographics: Women develop PTSD approximately 2× more often than men following equivalent trauma; combat veterans, refugees, and sexual assault survivors carry the highest rates (15-35%)
keyStats.avgAge: Can develop at any age; peak incidence in young adulthood (18-35) following combat, assault, or accidents; childhood onset associated with more complex presentations
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 350-400 million people have PTSD globally at any given time (WHO GBD 2019)

definition: Post-traumatic stress disorder (ICD-11: 6B40; DSM-5: 309.81) is a trauma- and stressor-related disorder that develops after direct or indirect exposure to a traumatic event involving actual or threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence. DSM-5 requires symptoms lasting more than one month across four clusters: (1) intrusion — flashbacks, nightmares, distressing trauma-related memories; (2) avoidance of trauma-related stimuli; (3) negative alterations in cognition and mood — trauma-related amnesia, distorted self-blame, persistent negative emotions, anhedonia; and (4) marked alterations in arousal and reactivity — hypervigilance, exaggerated startle, sleep disturbance, reckless behaviour, irritability. Neurobiologically, PTSD involves dysregulation of the fear network: hyperactivation of the amygdala, reduced hippocampal volume (associated with impaired fear extinction), and decreased prefrontal cortical inhibition of threat-related amygdala activation. HPA-axis dysregulation with lower-than-normal cortisol in some phenotypes distinguishes PTSD from uncomplicated stress responses. ICD-11 additionally recognises complex PTSD (CPTSD) as a distinct diagnosis when trauma is prolonged or repeated and additional disturbances in affect regulation, self-perception, and interpersonal function are present. PTSD is managed by psychiatrists and clinical psychologists trained in trauma-focused therapies.

typesClassification.description: Standard PTSD following a single or multiple traumatic events; the four DSM-5 symptom clusters (intrusion, avoidance, negative cognitions/mood, hyperarousal) must all be present and cause clinically significant impairment for at least one month.

Distinct ICD-11 diagnosis for PTSD following prolonged or repeated trauma (prolonged abuse, war captivity, trafficking). Includes all standard PTSD criteria plus disturbances in self-organisation: affect dysregulation, negative self-concept, and interpersonal difficulties. Recognised in ICD-11 but not as a separate DSM-5 category.

PTSD-like symptoms arising within 3 days to 4 weeks of trauma exposure; distinguishable from PTSD only by duration. ASD predicts PTSD in approximately 50% of cases. Brief trauma-focused CBT delivered within the first month reduces conversion to PTSD.

DSM-5 specifier for PTSD patients experiencing prominent depersonalisation (feeling detached from own body/thoughts) or derealisation (world feels unreal). More common in individuals with childhood trauma; requires modification of standard trauma-focused therapy.

Presents differently from adult PTSD: re-experiencing includes trauma-specific re-enactment play, frightening dreams without recognisable content, and trauma-specific re-enactment rather than flashbacks. Diagnosis uses modified DSM-5 developmental criteria.

primarySymptoms: Involuntary, distressing flashbacks in which the trauma feels as though it is happening again in the present — often triggered by sensory cues (smells, sounds, locations) associated with the original event.
Recurrent, distressing trauma-related nightmares that disrupt sleep and cause the person to fear going to bed, creating a cycle of sleep deprivation that worsens daytime symptoms.
Intense psychological distress or physiological reactivity (heart racing, sweating, trembling) when exposed to internal or external cues that symbolise or resemble the traumatic event.
Persistent and effortful avoidance of memories, thoughts, or feelings associated with the trauma, often by staying constantly busy, using substances, or restricting activities.
Avoidance of external reminders — people, places, conversations, activities, objects, or situations that arouse trauma-related distress; can progressively restrict daily life.
Inability to recall important aspects of the traumatic event due to dissociative amnesia — distinct from ordinary forgetting.
Persistent exaggerated negative beliefs about oneself ('I am damaged'), others ('People cannot be trusted'), or the world ('Nowhere is safe') that emerged or worsened after the trauma.
Markedly diminished interest in previously enjoyed activities, emotional numbing, and persistent inability to experience positive emotions such as happiness, love, or satisfaction.
Hypervigilance — a sustained state of heightened alertness to threat, scanning the environment constantly, difficulty relaxing even in objectively safe settings.
Exaggerated startle response, irritability, angry outbursts often out of proportion to the provocation, and reckless or self-destructive behaviour driven by emotional dysregulation.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent distressing dreams or sleep difficulties that begin within days of a traumatic event and do not improve after the first week.
Intrusive trauma-related memories or unwanted mental images arising repeatedly in the hours or days after exposure — if still present at one month, PTSD screening is indicated.
Complete emotional shutdown or numbness immediately after a traumatic event (peritraumatic dissociation) — this is among the strongest predictors of subsequent PTSD.
The newly traumatised person begins reorganising their life to avoid triggers (refusing to drive, leaving a job, stopping social activities) within weeks of the event.
emergencySigns: Active suicidal ideation or a suicide attempt — PTSD carries a 6-fold increased risk of attempted suicide compared with the trauma-exposed general population.
Acute dissociative episode with significant disorientation or loss of awareness of surroundings, creating risk of accidental self-harm.
Severe domestic or community violence by a person with PTSD-related anger dysregulation — safety planning and immediate psychiatric review required.
Acute psychotic break with paranoid ideation or auditory hallucinations — distinguishing PTSD with severe hyperarousal and hypervigilance from true psychosis requires urgent psychiatric assessment.

causes.description: Experiencing combat, sexual assault, physical violence, serious accidents, natural disasters, or life-threatening medical diagnoses are the most potent exposures. Sexual assault carries the highest conditional risk of PTSD of any trauma type: approximately 45-65% of women assaulted develop PTSD (Kessler et al. 1995).

causes.cause: Witnessing trauma or learning of trauma to close others
causes.description: Witnessing death, serious injury, or violence to others — including first-responders, healthcare workers, and bystanders — produces PTSD in 5-15% of witnesses, substantially less than direct exposure. Learning that a close relative experienced violent or accidental death can also trigger PTSD.

Traumatic events produce extreme activation of the fear network. In PTSD, the amygdala remains hyperactive and fails to extinguish conditioned fear responses. The hippocampus, critical for contextual fear memory, shows reduced volume (5-10% smaller in PTSD vs controls). HPA-axis dysregulation produces atypically low cortisol in many patients, impairing the normal suppression of the fear response.

Twin studies estimate PTSD heritability at 30-40%. Polymorphisms in FKBP5 (glucocorticoid receptor gene), BDNF, and serotonin transporter (5-HTTLPR) genes moderate PTSD risk after trauma. Childhood trauma epigenetically modifies glucocorticoid receptor gene methylation, raising lifetime PTSD vulnerability.

Depression, anxiety disorders, and prior trauma history each increase conditional PTSD risk approximately 2-fold after a new traumatic exposure. Substance use disorder doubles PTSD risk and substantially complicates treatment.

Perceived lack of social support after trauma is one of the strongest modifiable predictors of PTSD. Meta-analyses show that higher post-trauma social support is associated with a 25-40% reduced PTSD incidence regardless of trauma severity.

riskFactors.description: Women develop PTSD at approximately twice the rate of men following equivalent trauma across all event types. Hormonal, neurobiological, and social factors contribute; differences in trauma type (higher rates of sexual trauma in women) account for part of the disparity.
Dose-response relationship between trauma severity, duration, and PTSD risk. Prolonged or repeated trauma, direct harm, and sexual violation carry the highest conditional risk.
Adverse childhood experiences (ACEs) double PTSD risk after subsequent trauma in adulthood; early trauma epigenetically modifies the stress response system, increasing vulnerability.
Isolation and lack of supportive relationships after trauma are among the most consistent predictors of PTSD onset and chronicity; early social connection and peer support reduce risk.

Feeling detached, emotionally numb, or experiencing time distortion at the time of trauma (peritraumatic dissociation) predicts PTSD in 40-60% of those who experience it, making it one of the strongest acute predictors.
Military personnel, police, firefighters, paramedics, and emergency-department staff have PTSD rates of 10-25%, substantially above the general population due to repeated traumatic exposures.
Prior history of depression or anxiety disorder approximately doubles conditional PTSD risk after new traumatic exposure; existing biological vulnerability is compounded by the new trauma.

affectedDemographics: Military veterans: PTSD rates of 10-30% in post-9/11 veterans (RAND Corporation 2008); Vietnam-era veterans show lifetime rates up to 30%.
Refugees and conflict-affected populations: PTSD prevalence 15-30% in refugees from active conflict zones (Fazel et al. 2005 systematic review).
Sexual assault survivors: approximately 45-65% of assault survivors develop PTSD, the highest conditional risk of any single trauma category.

Children in conflict or disaster zones: children exposed to war or natural disasters show PTSD rates of 30-40%; symptom presentation differs from adults and includes regression, separation anxiety, and re-enactment play.
Healthcare and emergency workers: 10-25% PTSD prevalence; COVID-19 pandemic associated with significantly elevated rates in ICU and emergency-department staff.
diagnosisOverview: PTSD diagnosis is clinical, based on DSM-5 or ICD-11 criteria established through structured interview. The Clinician-Administered PTSD Scale for DSM-5 (CAPS-5) is the gold-standard diagnostic and severity tool, with scores above 33 indicating moderate-to-severe PTSD. The PCL-5 (PTSD Checklist for DSM-5) is a validated 20-item self-report screening measure with a cut-off score of 33 in civilian populations. Diagnosis requires clear identification of a Criterion A traumatic event, followed by one or more intrusion symptoms, one or more avoidance symptoms, two or more negative cognition/mood symptoms, and two or more hyperarousal symptoms, all lasting more than one month and causing significant functional impairment. The key differentials are: acute stress disorder (same symptoms but duration less than one month), adjustment disorder (milder reaction to a stressor without the specific intrusion and hyperarousal cluster), complex PTSD (ICD-11, with additional self-organisation disturbance), dissociative disorders, major depression, borderline personality disorder, and traumatic brain injury (where cognitive symptoms may mimic PTSD but lack the specific intrusion cluster). Neuroimaging is not routinely indicated. Medical evaluation excludes traumatic brain injury, which co-occurs with PTSD in up to 30% of combat veterans and requires independent management.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard clinician-administered diagnostic interview and severity rating for PTSD per DSM-5; score guides treatment intensity
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A structured 45-60 minute interview covering 20 DSM-5 PTSD symptoms. Each symptom rated on frequency (0-4) and intensity (0-4). A total score above 33 indicates clinically significant PTSD. Used at baseline and after each treatment phase to track response.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Self-report screening and symptom monitoring; identifies probable PTSD and tracks treatment response between sessions
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 20-item questionnaire completed in 5 minutes. Items rated 0 (not at all) to 4 (extremely). A score above 33 in civilian populations or 38 in military/veteran samples is clinically meaningful. Can be completed repeatedly to monitor treatment progress.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms PTSD diagnosis and screens for comorbidities including major depression, substance use disorder, bipolar disorder, and personality disorder
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 60-90 minute interview covering multiple diagnostic domains. Used in specialist settings rather than primary care; essential before initiating trauma-focused therapy to exclude contraindications.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents childhood trauma history and severity, informing PTSD complexity, treatment intensity, and risk of complex PTSD
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A brief 10-item self-report questionnaire assessing childhood abuse, neglect, and household dysfunction. Takes 5 minutes; results used to contextualise the trauma narrative rather than confirm diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes PTSD cognitive symptoms (concentration, memory) from traumatic brain injury or dementia in veterans and assault survivors
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30-60 minute battery including tests of attention, memory, and processing speed. Particularly important in veterans with blast-exposure history.

treatmentOverview: PTSD treatment is guided by the APA 2017, NICE NG116 (2018), VA/DoD 2023, and ISTSS 2019 guidelines, all of which give the highest evidence rating to trauma-focused psychological therapies. Three therapies have Level A (strong) evidence: prolonged exposure (PE), cognitive processing therapy (CPT), and eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing (EMDR). PE involves systematic imaginal and in vivo exposure to trauma memories and avoidance triggers. CPT targets distorted trauma-related cognitions about safety, trust, power, esteem, and intimacy. EMDR uses bilateral sensory stimulation during brief trauma memory processing to reduce emotional intensity. All three produce equivalent outcomes in head-to-head trials, with remission rates of 50-70% over 8-20 sessions. For patients unable or unwilling to engage with trauma-focused therapy, the first-line pharmacological options are SSRIs (sertraline and paroxetine are the only FDA-approved drugs for PTSD) and SNRIs (venlafaxine). SSRIs and SNRIs reduce the three major symptom clusters but have lower remission rates than trauma-focused therapy (40-60% symptom reduction). Prazosin, an alpha-1 blocker, is used adjunctively for PTSD-related nightmares. Combined treatment (therapy plus pharmacotherapy) does not consistently outperform trauma-focused therapy alone but is recommended when depression is co-morbid or therapy response is partial. Non-pharmacological adjuncts with supporting evidence include MDMA-assisted therapy (Phase 3 RCTs underway, not yet approved), ketamine infusion, and hypnotherapy in specialist settings.

medicalTreatments.name: Sertraline (25-200 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: FDA-approved for PTSD; first-line SSRI. Reduces re-experiencing, avoidance, and hyperarousal symptom clusters. Titrate over 4 weeks to the effective dose; full PTSD response requires 10-12 weeks. Preferred in patients with comorbid depression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 40-60% symptom reduction versus placebo in RCTs; response rate 45-65% in PTSD-specific trials

medicalTreatments.name: Paroxetine (20-60 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: The second FDA-approved SSRI for PTSD. Equal efficacy to sertraline; higher rates of discontinuation syndrome on stopping due to short half-life. Useful when insomnia or anxious arousal is prominent.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rate approximately 50-60% in pivotal RCTs; comparable to sertraline in head-to-head data

medicalTreatments.name: Venlafaxine extended-release (75-225 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: SNRI with evidence across all four PTSD symptom clusters; guideline-recommended alternative to SSRIs. Approved for PTSD in several European countries. Particularly useful when depression and PTSD coexist.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Y-BOCS equivalent PTSD scale reduction of 30-40% versus placebo in the Davidson et al. 2006 RCT

medicalTreatments.name: Prazosin (1-15 mg at bedtime)
medicalTreatments.description: Alpha-1 adrenergic antagonist; reduces noradrenergic drive on the amygdala during REM sleep. Used adjunctively for PTSD nightmares unresponsive to trauma-focused therapy or SSRI. Start at 1 mg and titrate slowly over weeks to avoid first-dose hypotension.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significant reduction in nightmare frequency and severity in multiple RCTs (Taylor et al. 2008, Raskind et al. 2018); effect size 0.5-0.8

medicalTreatments.name: Trauma-focused CBT / Prolonged Exposure (PE)
medicalTreatments.description: 16-session structured protocol involving psychoeducation, breathing retraining, repeated imaginal exposure to the trauma memory, and in vivo exposure to avoidance triggers. Delivered weekly in 60-90 minute sessions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Remission in 40-60% of completers; Y-BOCS equivalent reduction of 50-70% versus waitlist; one of the most effective brief treatments in psychiatry

medicalTreatments.name: Eye Movement Desensitisation and Reprocessing (EMDR)
medicalTreatments.description: Structured 8-phase protocol in which brief exposures to trauma memories are paired with bilateral eye movements or tapping. Active component may be the brief focused memory processing rather than the bilateral stimulation specifically. 8-12 sessions for single-incident trauma.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Equivalent to PE and CPT in head-to-head trials; large effect sizes versus waitlist (d=1.0-1.5) in meta-analyses

alternativeTreatments.name: MDMA-assisted therapy (Phase 3 investigational)
alternativeTreatments.description: MDMA (given in 2-3 supervised sessions alongside psychotherapy) produced 67% PTSD remission versus 32% for therapy alone in the MAPS Phase 3 RCT (Mitchell et al. 2021, PMID 34021146). Not yet approved by FDA or EMA but breakthrough therapy designation granted.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR) as adjunct
alternativeTreatments.description: An 8-week MBSR programme reduces PTSD hyperarousal and negative-cognition clusters. A 2015 RCT (Polusny et al. JAMA) found MBSR non-inferior to present-centred therapy for veteran PTSD, though inferior to PE for full remission.

whySeeSpecialist: Specialist psychiatric or clinical psychology input is essential to deliver trauma-focused therapy correctly and to manage comorbid depression, substance use, suicidality, and complex PTSD presentations. Generic supportive counselling without a trauma-focused protocol produces significantly inferior outcomes to PE, CPT, or EMDR delivered by trained therapists.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a psychologist or psychiatrist with formal training in at least one of PE, CPT, or EMDR and a caseload primarily comprising trauma patients. The EMDR International Association and the Association for Behavioral and Cognitive Therapies maintain therapist directories. Verify that any therapist uses CAPS-5 or PCL-5 to monitor treatment progress.
hospitalCriteria: Active suicidality requiring inpatient safety monitoring
Severe dissociation or functional collapse preventing engagement with outpatient therapy
Comorbid severe substance use disorder requiring medically supervised detoxification alongside PTSD stabilisation
Complex PTSD with risk of severe self-harm or interpersonal violence
Residential or day-programme PTSD services for treatment-resistant cases
keyFacilities: Specialist trauma-focused outpatient psychology or psychiatry clinics
VA medical centres and military veteran mental health services
Child and adolescent mental health services (CAMHS) with trauma specialisation
preventionStrategies: Psychological first aid (PFA) delivered within hours to days of mass trauma events reduces acute distress without pathologising normal reactions; does not prevent PTSD on its own but is standard WHO-recommended post-disaster response.
Brief trauma-focused CBT (5-12 sessions) beginning 2-4 weeks after trauma in symptomatic individuals reduces PTSD onset by approximately 50% in RCTs (Kornor et al. 2008 Cochrane review).

Ensure adequate social support systems for high-risk populations (veterans, trauma surgeons, police) — peer-support programmes reduce PTSD incidence by maintaining social connection in the immediate post-trauma period.
Screen high-risk occupational groups (military, emergency services) regularly using PCL-5 and enable early access to trauma-focused therapy before symptoms become entrenched.
Prevent interpersonal violence and child abuse — the two trauma categories most reliably associated with PTSD development and the most amenable to population-level prevention.
lifestyleModifications: Engage actively and consistently with assigned trauma-focused therapy sessions; PTSD that is partially engaged with (avoiding the most distressing exposures) maintains avoidance and limits recovery.
Establish a regular sleep schedule and bedtime routine; PTSD-related insomnia perpetuates hyperarousal — prazosin and sleep hygiene should be addressed in parallel with trauma processing.
Reduce alcohol and substance use — substances suppress but do not process trauma memories, increase hyperarousal rebound, and worsen nightmares and emotional dysregulation.
Maintain social connections even when avoidance drives withdrawal — planned, low-pressure social contact counteracts PTSD-driven isolation.
Regular aerobic exercise (150 minutes per week) reduces PTSD symptom severity and hyperarousal — a 2014 meta-analysis found effect sizes of 0.5-0.8 for exercise on PTSD symptom reduction.
Use a safety plan for suicidal ideation or self-harm urges — keep the plan visible and share crisis contacts with a trusted support person.

recommended: Regular meals at consistent times to stabilise blood glucose and reduce irritability and hyperarousal
Omega-3 fatty acids (fish, walnuts, flaxseed) — early evidence from a 2010 Japanese RCT (Matsuoka et al.) suggests EPA+DHA supplementation may reduce PTSD symptom development after acute trauma
Magnesium-rich foods (nuts, seeds, whole grains) to modulate HPA-axis hyperactivation and reduce anxiety
Tryptophan-containing protein sources (turkey, eggs, dairy) to support serotonin synthesis
avoid: Alcohol — worsens sleep architecture, REM disruption, and increases emotional dysregulation; strongly associated with PTSD chronicity
Caffeine above 200 mg/day — heightens physiological arousal and worsens hypervigilance and sleep difficulties
Cannabis — despite short-term anxiolytic effect, regular use is associated with worse PTSD outcomes in longitudinal studies
Irregular or skipped meals, particularly in patients on SSRIs, which can disrupt tolerability

exerciseGuidelines: 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic exercise per week is recommended as an adjunct to trauma-focused therapy for PTSD. Exercise reduces amygdala hyperactivation, promotes hippocampal neurogenesis, and lowers HPA-axis reactivity — the three core neurobiological abnormalities in PTSD. Yoga with trauma-sensitive modifications (Emerson & Hopper protocol) shows additional benefit for body-awareness and dissociation reduction.
dailyManagement: Complete any between-session exposure tasks assigned by your therapist; avoidance between sessions erodes the previous session's gains.
Use grounding techniques (5-4-3-2-1 sensory method, cold water, slow diaphragmatic breathing) during flashbacks or hyperarousal spikes to return to the present moment.
Take prescribed medication consistently; SSRI or venlafaxine doses should not be stopped abruptly — taper under medical supervision to avoid withdrawal and relapse.
Track daily symptom score using PCL-5 every 2 weeks to monitor trajectory and provide data for clinician review.
Maintain a brief sleep diary; discuss persistent sleep disturbance with your clinician as prazosin or targeted CBT-I may be needed in addition to trauma therapy.
Inform close family members or trusted friends about PTSD symptoms and safety-plan commitments so they can support monitoring.
prognosis: PTSD outcomes depend strongly on treatment access and type. With evidence-based trauma-focused therapy, 50-70% of patients achieve remission (CAPS-5 below 22) over 8-20 sessions. Untreated, approximately 40% of PTSD cases become chronic and persist for years or decades; population-based studies report median duration of 36 months in treated versus over 64 months in untreated individuals (Kessler 1995). Predictors of poorer prognosis include childhood-onset trauma, complex PTSD, comorbid substance use disorder, chronic pain, TBI, and lack of social support. Suicide risk is elevated throughout the illness: PTSD carries a 6-fold increased risk of suicide attempt versus the general population, predominantly driven by comorbid depression and hopelessness. With sustained multimodal treatment, many patients achieve substantial functional recovery and return to work; full symptomatic remission is achieved in 30-40% of those treated in real-world (non-trial) settings.
recoveryTimeline: Significant symptom reduction is typically evident after 3-6 sessions of PE, CPT, or EMDR. Most clinical trials demonstrate primary endpoint response by 8-12 sessions. SSRIs require 10-12 weeks to achieve full PTSD symptom reduction. For complex PTSD, stabilisation and skills-building phases may add 3-6 months before trauma-processing begins. Maintenance pharmacotherapy and booster therapy sessions are recommended for 12-24 months after remission to reduce relapse.
complications: Major depression — occurs in approximately 50% of individuals with PTSD and markedly increases suicide risk; must be assessed and treated concurrently.
Substance use disorder — 30-50% of PTSD patients develop problematic alcohol or drug use as self-medication; co-morbid SUD requires integrated dual-diagnosis treatment.

Chronic pain and somatic disorders — PTSD is associated with significantly elevated rates of fibromyalgia, irritable bowel syndrome, and chronic headache, reflecting shared neurobiological mechanisms.

Cardiovascular disease — a 2018 meta-analysis found that PTSD is associated with a 1.5-2-fold elevated risk of coronary artery disease and cardiovascular mortality, likely mediated by HPA-axis dysregulation and poor health behaviours.
Occupational disability and homelessness — severe PTSD causes job loss, homelessness, and poverty; veterans with untreated PTSD are over-represented in homeless populations.

Relationship breakdown and family impact — PTSD symptoms (irritability, emotional numbing, avoidance) severely strain intimate partnerships; children of parents with PTSD show elevated rates of anxiety and behavioural difficulties.

supportResources.name: PTSD Alliance / PTSD UK
supportResources.description: UK charity providing free evidence-based information, peer forums, and specialist PTSD therapist signposting.

supportResources.name: National Center for PTSD (VA)
supportResources.description: US Veterans Affairs national resource for patients, families, and clinicians with treatment guides, PCL-5 scoring tools, and research updates.

supportResources.name: NICE NG116: Post-traumatic stress disorder
supportResources.description: UK guideline on evidence-based PTSD diagnosis and treatment, freely accessible to patients and clinicians.

supportResources.name: MAPS MDMA-Assisted Therapy Information
supportResources.description: Non-profit organisation conducting Phase 3 trials of MDMA-assisted therapy; provides current study information for clinicians and prospective patients.

confusedWithConditions.name: Generalised Anxiety Disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: GAD is characterised by chronic, wide-ranging worry about multiple life domains without a specific precipitating trauma and without flashbacks, intrusive trauma memories, or avoidance of trauma cues. PTSD requires a Criterion A traumatic event and specific intrusion symptoms. The two conditions can co-occur, but treatment differs: GAD responds to standard CBT without trauma exposure.

confusedWithConditions.name: Major Depressive Disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Depression causes anhedonia, low mood, and cognitive slowing that overlap with the negative-cognition cluster of PTSD. MDD lacks the intrusion and re-experiencing symptoms (flashbacks, trauma nightmares) and the hyperarousal cluster. When both are present, addressing the trauma component often improves depression as well.

confusedWithConditions.name: OCD
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both involve intrusive mental content and avoidance, but OCD obsessions are ego-dystonic thoughts about harm, contamination, or symmetry unrelated to a specific past trauma. PTSD intrusions are trauma-specific, sensory-laden flashbacks or nightmares. When trauma precipitates OCD onset, both diagnoses may apply.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bipolar Disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: PTSD emotional dysregulation, hyperarousal, and irritability can resemble hypomania, while the numbing and anhedonia phases can resemble depression. Bipolar disorder requires distinct episodic mood states with clear onset and offset, not chronic trauma-related symptom fluctuation. Misdiagnosis risks inappropriate mood-stabiliser prescribing without trauma therapy.

relatedConditions.name: OCD
relatedConditions.relevance: Both involve intrusive mental content and avoidance behaviour driven by anxiety; they share overlapping CBT treatment principles while requiring distinct protocols.

relatedConditions.name: Depression
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-occurs in approximately 50% of PTSD cases; both disorders share dysregulated HPA-axis and serotonin systems and are treated with overlapping pharmacotherapy.

relatedConditions.name: Anxiety Disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Generalised anxiety frequently co-occurs with PTSD and shares hyperarousal as a symptom; distinguishing the two guides treatment selection.

relatedConditions.name: Bipolar Disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Comorbid PTSD and bipolar disorder is common and complicates treatment planning; mood stabilisation must precede or accompany trauma-focused therapy.

faqs.question: What is PTSD in simple terms?
faqs.answer: PTSD is a psychiatric disorder that develops in about 20% of people after experiencing or witnessing a traumatic event. The brain's fear system becomes stuck on high alert, producing flashbacks, nightmares, hypervigilance, avoidance, and emotional numbing that persist for months to years. It is treatable with trauma-focused therapy and medication.

faqs.question: What are the four types of PTSD symptoms?
faqs.answer: DSM-5 groups PTSD symptoms into four clusters: intrusion (flashbacks, nightmares), avoidance (avoiding memories or reminders), negative alterations in cognition and mood (distorted beliefs, emotional numbing), and hyperarousal (hypervigilance, exaggerated startle, sleep disturbance, irritability). All four clusters must be present for a PTSD diagnosis.

faqs.question: How is PTSD diagnosed?
faqs.answer: PTSD is diagnosed by a psychiatrist or psychologist using DSM-5 criteria confirmed by a structured interview (CAPS-5) or validated screening tool (PCL-5). Diagnosis requires a Criterion A traumatic event and symptoms in all four clusters lasting more than one month and causing significant functional impairment. There is no blood test or brain scan that diagnoses PTSD.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for PTSD?
faqs.answer: Trauma-focused psychological therapies — prolonged exposure (PE), cognitive processing therapy (CPT), and EMDR — are the gold-standard treatments for PTSD with the highest evidence ratings from NICE, APA, and VA guidelines. They produce remission in 50-70% of patients. SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine) are first-line pharmacotherapy when therapy is unavailable or insufficient.

faqs.question: Can PTSD be cured?
faqs.answer: PTSD cannot always be cured but is highly treatable. Approximately 50-70% of patients achieve remission with evidence-based trauma-focused therapy. Many others achieve substantial functional improvement even without full symptom resolution. Without treatment, 40% of cases become chronic and persist for years.

faqs.question: How long does PTSD last without treatment?
faqs.answer: Without treatment, the median PTSD duration is approximately 64 months (over 5 years) in population-based studies. Approximately 40% of untreated cases become chronic. With evidence-based therapy, median duration falls to around 36 months and remission is achieved in the majority of cases within 12 months of starting treatment.

faqs.question: What medications treat PTSD?
faqs.answer: Sertraline and paroxetine are the only FDA-approved medications for PTSD. Venlafaxine (SNRI) is a guideline-recommended alternative. Prazosin is used adjunctively for nightmares. Medications reduce symptoms by 40-60% but are less effective than trauma-focused therapy for full remission.

faqs.question: What is EMDR therapy for PTSD?
faqs.answer: EMDR (eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing) is an 8-phase structured therapy where trauma memories are briefly recalled while the patient tracks bilateral eye movements or receives tactile stimulation. It reduces the emotional intensity of trauma memories and produces equivalent remission rates to prolonged exposure in head-to-head RCTs.

faqs.question: Is PTSD more common in women or men?
faqs.answer: Women develop PTSD at approximately twice the rate of men following equivalent traumatic exposures. This reflects higher rates of sexual trauma, differential neurobiological stress responses, and reporting differences. The lifetime prevalence is 9-12% in women and 4-5% in men in US population surveys.

faqs.question: What events cause PTSD?
faqs.answer: PTSD can follow any event involving actual or threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence. The highest-risk events are sexual assault (45-65% conditional risk), combat exposure (10-30%), and natural disasters or accidents (10-15%). Most traumatised people do not develop PTSD; individual vulnerability and post-trauma support determine who does.

faqs.question: Can children develop PTSD?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children develop PTSD after trauma exposure, though symptoms differ from adults: young children show re-enactment play, frightening dreams without trauma content, and separation anxiety rather than classic flashbacks. Trauma-focused CBT adapted for children (TF-CBT) is the evidence-based treatment.

faqs.question: Does PTSD increase the risk of suicide?
faqs.answer: Yes. PTSD is associated with a 6-fold increased risk of suicide attempt compared with the general population. Risk is highest when comorbid depression is present. Suicidal ideation should be assessed at every clinical contact in patients with PTSD.

faqs.question: What is the difference between PTSD and complex PTSD?
faqs.answer: Complex PTSD (CPTSD, ICD-11) includes all standard PTSD symptoms plus three additional disturbances in self-organisation: affect dysregulation, a severely negative self-concept, and persistent interpersonal difficulties. CPTSD typically follows prolonged or repeated trauma such as childhood abuse, trafficking, or war captivity.

faqs.question: Can PTSD develop years after a trauma?
faqs.answer: Yes. Delayed-onset PTSD, where full criteria are not met until at least 6 months after the traumatic event, occurs in approximately 25% of PTSD cases. A common pattern is subthreshold symptoms immediately post-trauma that escalate to full PTSD following a secondary stressor or life change.

faqs.question: How does PTSD affect the brain?
faqs.answer: PTSD involves amygdala hyperactivation producing persistent fear responses, reduced hippocampal volume impairing fear extinction, and decreased prefrontal cortical inhibition of threat processing. HPA-axis dysregulation results in atypically low cortisol in many patients. Successful trauma therapy partially reverses these neurobiological changes.

metaDescription: PTSD: trauma-related disorder with flashbacks, nightmares and hyperarousal affecting 3.9% of adults. Evidence-based therapy (EMDR, PE) and medication reviewed.
